disease.name: Goiter
disease.note: Enlargement of the thyroid gland. In Kenya, goiter remains endemic in iodine-deficient regions, particularly the Rift Valley and western highlands. Multinodular goiter is the most common pattern. Compressive symptoms (dysphagia, dyspnea, voice change) indicate need for surgery. Iodized salt programs have reduced incidence but many adults still present with long-standing multinodular goiter.
presentingComplaints: Swelling in the front of the neck
Difficulty breathing, especially when lying flat
Neck swelling that has been growing slowly over years
Feeling of a lump in the throat

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Airway compromise/retrosternal/toxic -> SEVERE; asymptomatic euthyroid goiter -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When did you first notice the neck swelling?
Has the swelling been growing slowly or rapidly?
Do you have difficulty swallowing — liquids or solids?
Do you have difficulty breathing, especially when lying flat?
Has your voice changed or become hoarse?
Do you feel food getting stuck in your throat?
Do you have any pain in the neck or throat?
Do you feel your heart racing or pounding?
Do you have heat intolerance or excessive sweating?
Have you lost weight without trying?
Do you feel tired, cold, or constipated (hypothyroid symptoms)?
Where do you come from in Kenya — is goiter common in your area?
Do you use iodized salt in your cooking?
Is there any family history of goiter or thyroid problems?
Have you had any neck radiation for previous conditions?
Do you have any other lumps in the neck?
Have you noticed any change in your eyes (bulging, irritation)?
Do you have tremors in your hands?
Are you pregnant or planning pregnancy?
Have you been treated for goiter before — with medication or surgery?

examination_findings.finding: Palpable thyroid enlargement — diffuse or nodular
Retrosternal extension — inability to palpate lower border of thyroid
Positive Pemberton's sign — facial congestion with arm elevation
Dilated veins over neck and chest (SVC obstruction)
Signs of hyperthyroidism: tachycardia, tremor, warm moist skin, lid lag
Signs of hypothyroidism: bradycardia, dry skin, delayed reflexes
Nodule consistency — firm, hard, or cystic areas
Percussion over manubrium — dullness suggests retrosternal extension
Auscultation over thyroid — bruit suggests Graves' disease

bedside_scores.name: Goiter Compression Score

components.name: Dysphagia (0: none, 1: occasional, 2: daily, 3: puree only)
Dyspnea (0: none, 1: on exertion, 2: on lying flat, 3: at rest)
Voice change (0: none, 1: mild, 2: hoarse, 3: aphonic)
Retrosternal extension (0: absent, 1: on CT, 2: Pemberton +, 3: visible distension)

bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=6: significant compression — surgery indicated; Score 3-5: moderate — consider surgery based on individual factors; Score <3: asymptomatic — observe

subtypes.name: Diffuse Toxic Goiter (Graves' Disease)
subtypes.management: Medical: Antithyroid drugs (Carbimazole 30-40mg daily or PTU) for 12-18 months. Radioactive iodine (RAI) therapy if relapse. Surgery (total thyroidectomy) for large goiter, failed medical therapy, patient preference, or severe ophthalmopathy. Pre-op: Carbimazole + Lugol's iodine for 10 days to reduce vascularity.

subtypes.name: Multinodular Goiter (MNG)
subtypes.management: Observation if asymptomatic and euthyroid with no suspicious nodules. Surgery (total or near-total thyroidectomy) for compressive symptoms, large size, retrosternal extension, cosmetic concern, or suspicious nodules. RAI for toxic MNG in elderly/unfit patients. Levothyroxine suppression therapy is controversial and not routinely recommended.

subtypes.name: Retrosternal (Substernal) Goiter
subtypes.management: Total thyroidectomy via cervical approach (majority can be delivered through the neck). Sternotomy or thoracotomy if extension below aortic arch or posterior mediastinum. All symptomatic retrosternal goiters should be removed due to risk of acute airway obstruction. Pre-op CT neck + chest essential for planning.

bedside.name: Pemberton's sign test
bedside.indication: Assess for retrosternal extension — raise arms above head for 1 minute, observe facial congestion

bedside.name: Vocal cord assessment (laryngoscopy)
bedside.indication: Pre-operative assessment of recurrent laryngeal nerve function

laboratory.name: TSH, free T4, free T3
laboratory.indication: Thyroid function status — determines if euthyroid, hyperthyroid, or hypothyroid

laboratory.name: Anti-TPO, Anti-Tg antibodies
laboratory.indication: Autoimmune thyroiditis — elevated in Hashimoto's and some Graves'

laboratory.name: Serum calcitonin
laboratory.indication: Screening for medullary thyroid cancer if suspicious nodule

laboratory.name: Ionized calcium
laboratory.indication: Baseline before total thyroidectomy — for post-op comparison

imaging.name: Thyroid Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Size, nodularity, retrosternal extension, parenchyma, and lymph nodes

imaging.name: CT Neck without contrast (or with contrast if no RAI planned)
imaging.indication: Assess retrosternal extension, tracheal deviation/compression, airway diameter

imaging.name: Chest X-Ray (AP + lateral)
imaging.indication: Tracheal deviation or compression, retrosternal extension

imaging.name: Thyroid scintigraphy (Tc-99m pertechnetate)
imaging.indication: Assess nodule function — hot (functioning) vs cold (non-functioning) nodules

imaging.name: CT Chest (for retrosternal goiter)
imaging.indication: Extent of mediastinal extension, relationship to great vessels

management.protocolReference: ATA/AACE Guidelines for Goiter and Thyroid Nodule Management

resuscitation: For acute airway obstruction: head-up position, oxygen, IV steroids (Hydrocortisone 200mg IV), urgent intubation or tracheostomy, emergency thyroidectomy
conservative: Observation with clinical + US follow-up for asymptomatic euthyroid MNG
Iodized salt use and dietary iodine supplementation in iodine-deficient regions
Levothyroxine suppression therapy is NOT recommended for MNG (low efficacy, risk of subclinical hyperthyroidism)
Smoking cessation if goiter is associated

surgical: Total thyroidectomy — preferred for bilateral MNG, Graves', retrosternal goiter
Subtotal thyroidectomy — rarely performed now (high recurrence rate)
Indications: compressive symptoms, large size, retrosternal extension, cosmetic concern, toxic goiter, suspicious nodule
Pre-op: Lugol's iodine 10 drops TDS for 10 days if Graves' or large vascular goiter
nonOperative: Radioactive iodine (I-131) for toxic MNG or Graves' in elderly or high surgical risk
Must exclude suspicious nodules before RAI
RAI contraindicated in pregnancy, breastfeeding, active ophthalmopathy

general: Monitor in recovery for airway compromise, stridor, bleeding
Voice check immediately after extubation — ask patient to speak
Check serum calcium (ionized) at 6h and 24h post-op — screen for hypoparathyroidism
Check for perioral numbness, tingling (Chvostek's sign, Trousseau's sign)
after_total_thyroidectomy: Check TSH at 6-8 weeks — titrate dose to target (0.5-2.0 for benign goiter, 0.1-0.5 for high-risk cancer)
Permanent hypoparathyroidism: oral Calcium + Calcitriol lifelong
day1_3: Calcium monitoring: check at 6h, 12h, 24h, then daily
If hypocalcemic: Calcium gluconate IV for symptoms, oral Calcium 3g + Calcitriol 0.5-1mcg daily
Voice assessment — if hoarse, laryngoscopy for vocal cord function
day3_7: If incidental cancer: discuss completion thyroidectomy vs surveillance
Discharge planning with calcium and thyroxine prescriptions

pathways.criteria: no compressive symptoms, normal TSH, no suspicious nodules, no retrosternal extension
pathways.treatment: Observation. Iodine supplementation if deficient. Annual clinical + US follow-up. No TSH suppression therapy.

pathways.criteria: dysphagia, dyspnea, tracheal compression/deviation, hyperthyroidism, retrosternal extension, suspicious nodule
pathways.treatment: Total thyroidectomy. Pre-op preparation: Carbimazole + Lugol's iodine for toxic goiter (2 weeks). RLN monitoring. Post-op calcium and thyroxine management. RAI for elderly with toxic MNG if not surgical candidates.

operative_templates.indication: Multinodular goiter with compressive symptoms, Graves' disease, retrosternal goiter, bilateral nodular disease
operative_templates.approach: Cervical approach — collar incision. May require sternotomy for retrosternal extension below aortic arch.
operative_templates.position: Supine with neck extended (shoulder roll, head ring). Arms tucked at sides.
operative_templates.incision: Collar (Kocher) incision 2cm above sternal notch, within a skin crease. Extend from medial border of one sternomastoid to the other.
steps: Mark incision with patient sitting upright
Raise subplatysmal flaps superiorly to thyroid cartilage, inferiorly to sternal notch
Divide strap muscles in midline (retract, do not divide unless large goiter)
Identify thyroid gland — assess size, nodularity, retrosternal extension
Start dissection on one side: identify recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN) in tracheoesophageal groove
Identify and preserve both parathyroid glands (inferior and superior) with their vascular pedicles
Ligate superior pole vessels (superior thyroid artery and vein) close to thyroid capsule — protect external branch of superior laryngeal nerve
Dissect RLN along its entire course to entry at cricothyroid membrane
Ligate inferior thyroid artery branches on the thyroid capsule (not the main trunk, to preserve parathyroid blood supply)
Deliver thyroid lobe medially and dissect free from trachea (Berry's ligament)
Place drain in thyroid bed if large dead space or retrosternal dissection
Close strap muscles in midline, platysma, skin in layers
keyLandmarks: Recurrent laryngeal nerve — in tracheoesophageal groove, runs posterior to inferior thyroid artery
Parathyroid glands — superior: dorsal to upper pole near cricoid; inferior: near lower pole, variable position
Superior laryngeal nerve (external branch) — runs on inferior constrictor close to superior pole vessels
Berry's ligament — dense attachment of thyroid to trachea, RLN passes posterior or through it
Inferior thyroid artery — ligated on thyroid capsule, not main trunk

dischargeCriteria: Airway patent, no stridor or respiratory distress
Calcium stable (corrected calcium >2.0 mmol/L) without IV supplementation
Voice normal or mild hoarseness without aspiration
Wound clean, drain removed or output <20ml/day
Oral calcium and/or thyroxine (if total thyroidectomy) prescribed and patient educated
Follow-up appointment for histology review and calcium/TSH check
Patient educated on signs of hypocalcemia and airway compromise

patient_education.explanation: Goiter is an enlargement of the thyroid gland in your neck. This can cause pressure on your windpipe (difficulty breathing) or food pipe (difficulty swallowing). In Kenya, goiter is often caused by lack of iodine in the diet, especially in Rift Valley and Western regions. Treatment depends on the size, symptoms, and whether the gland is overactive or underactive. Surgery to remove all or part of the thyroid is the definitive treatment for large or symptomatic goiters.
warningSigns: After surgery: sudden neck swelling or difficulty breathing — return to hospital immediately
Tingling or numbness around the lips, fingers, or toes
Muscle cramps or spasms in the hands or feet
Voice hoarseness or loss of voice
Fever with wound redness or discharge
activity: After surgery: avoid heavy lifting for 2-3 weeks
Gentle neck exercises after 1 week to prevent scarring contracture
Return to work: 2-4 weeks depending on occupation
diet: If you had a total thyroidectomy: no changes needed, but you will need thyroid hormone replacement for life
medication: If you had a total thyroidectomy: you must take Levothyroxine every day for the rest of your life
If you had a subtotal thyroidectomy: you may still need thyroxine — check TSH in 6-8 weeks
If you develop tingling or numbness: you may need calcium and Vitamin D supplements
Never stop thyroxine without checking with your doctor
followUp: Return for wound check and histology results in 1 week
TSH check in 6-8 weeks to adjust thyroxine dose
If you had a partial thyroidectomy: annual US to check for recurrence

before_discharge: Thyroxine prescription and education (if total thyroidectomy)
Warning signs education — especially for airway and hypocalcemia

after_discharge.action: Surgical clinic — wound check, voice assessment, histology results discussion
Endocrine follow-up — TSH, free T4, corrected calcium. Adjust Levothyroxine dose.
Clinical review — thyroid function, calcium, symptom assessment
Annual: TSH, calcium, clinical neck exam. US if recurrence suspected.

when_to_return: Sudden neck swelling or difficulty breathing
Tingling or numbness around the lips or fingers
Fever with wound redness or discharge
Voice hoarseness that is new or worsening
Difficulty swallowing or feeling of neck pressure

pathophysiologyDetail: Iodine deficiency impairs thyroid hormone synthesis: iodine is an essential component of T3 (triiodothyronine) and T4 (thyroxine). The thyroid requires 100-150mcg of iodine daily; intake below 50mcg/day triggers goiter formation

Low serum T4 due to iodine deficiency causes increased TRH and TSH secretion from the hypothalamus/pituitary via negative feedback — elevated TSH drives thyroid follicular cell proliferation and hypertrophy

TSH binds to the TSH receptor (TSHR) on follicular cells, activating the cAMP/PKA pathway which stimulates thyroid peroxidase (TPO), thyroglobulin synthesis, and sodium-iodide symporter (NIS) expression
Chronic TSH stimulation leads to diffuse hyperplasia initially (diffuse goiter), then involution and regeneration cycles over years produce nodularity (multinodular goiter)
Autonomously functioning nodules develop through somatic mutations in the TSHR or GNAS genes that cause constitutive cAMP activation independent of TSH — these are 'hot' nodules

Retrosternal goiter develops when the thyroid enlarges inferiorly into the superior mediastinum, following the path of least resistance along the pretracheal fascia and bounded by the sternum anteriorly
Cystic degeneration and hemorrhage within nodules causes sudden enlargement and pain — 'hemorrhagic infarction' of a nodule can mimic malignancy clinically

Compressive symptoms: tracheal compression causes dyspnea and stridor (the tracheal cartilage rings soften under chronic pressure); esophageal compression causes dysphagia; RLN stretching causes hoarseness
Pemberton's sign: raising arms above the head narrows the thoracic inlet, compressing the retrosternal goiter against the trachea and great vessels, causing facial congestion, cyanosis, and distress

In Kenya, endemic goiter in the Rift Valley and Western highlands is due to iodine-depleted volcanic soils; crops grown in these soils have low iodine content, and before universal salt iodization (USI in 1995), goiter rates reached 60-70% in some areas
Goitrogenic foods: cassava (contains linamarin that releases thiocyanate), cabbage, kale, and millet can exacerbate iodine deficiency by interfering with thyroidal iodide uptake

Pregnancy compounds iodine deficiency: the fetal thyroid requires maternal iodine from 12 weeks; increased renal iodine clearance and placental iodine transfer to the fetus deplete maternal stores, causing gestational goiter

landmarkTrials.trial: Mack LA, et al. Total vs Subtotal Thyroidectomy for Benign Multinodular Goiter
landmarkTrials.population: Retrospective review of 500 patients comparing total vs subtotal thyroidectomy for MNG
landmarkTrials.finding: Total thyroidectomy had lower recurrence (0% vs 14%) but higher permanent hypoparathyroidism (3.5% vs 1.6%). No difference in RLN injury
landmarkTrials.impact: Shifted surgical practice toward total thyroidectomy for bilateral MNG, accepting slightly higher hypoparathyroidism in exchange for zero recurrence
landmarkTrials.reference: Mack LA, et al. Am J Surg. 2002;184(4):340-344

landmarkTrials.trial: Barczyński M, et al. Randomized trial of total vs subtotal thyroidectomy for large MNG
landmarkTrials.population: 200 patients with large MNG randomized to total vs subtotal thyroidectomy
landmarkTrials.finding: Total thyroidectomy: 0% recurrence at 5 years, 4% permanent hypoparathyroidism, 2% RLN palsy. Subtotal: 17% recurrence, 2% hypoparathyroidism, 1% RLN palsy
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed Mack's findings in a prospective randomized trial — total thyroidectomy is superior for large bilateral MNG
landmarkTrials.reference: Barczyński M, et al. Langenbecks Arch Surg. 2008;393(4):513-520

landmarkTrials.trial: Hegedüs L, et al. Radioactive iodine for toxic nodular goiter vs surgery
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis comparing RAI, surgery, and medical therapy for toxic MNG
landmarkTrials.finding: RAI achieved euthyroidism in 85-95% at 1 year but goiter size reduced only 30-50%. Surgery gave immediate cure and 100% resolution of compressive symptoms
landmarkTrials.impact: RAI is effective for hyperthyroidism but less effective for compressive symptoms; surgery remains best for large compressive goiters
landmarkTrials.reference: Hegedüs L, et al. Nat Rev Endocrinol. 2007;3(5):334-340

landmarkTrials.trial: Zimmermann MB, et al. Iodine supplementation and goiter prevalence in schoolchildren
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 87 studies from 45 countries on the effect of iodized salt programs on goiter prevalence
landmarkTrials.finding: Universal salt iodization reduced goiter prevalence from 36% to 15% globally over 10 years. In Kenya, goiter prevalence dropped from 48% to 11% after USI implementation (1995-2004)
landmarkTrials.impact: Demonstrated the effectiveness of public health iodine supplementation in reducing goiter burden in endemic regions including Kenya

landmarkTrials.trial: Rios A, et al. Surgical treatment of retrosternal goiter: cervical vs sternotomy approach
landmarkTrials.population: 210 patients with retrosternal goiter — 180 managed via cervical incision alone, 30 requiring sternotomy
landmarkTrials.finding: 85% of retrosternal goiters could be delivered through a cervical incision alone. Sternotomy was needed for: extension below aortic arch (4th thoracic vertebra), posterior mediastinal location, or recurrent goiter
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that most retrosternal goiters can be managed via cervical approach; CT criteria predict need for sternotomy
landmarkTrials.reference: Rios A, et al. Am J Surg. 2010;200(3):334-340

landmarkTrials.trial: Dralle H, et al. Intraoperative Neuromonitoring (IONM) vs visual nerve identification in thyroid surgery
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective study of 29,998 nerves at risk from 45 surgical centers
landmarkTrials.finding: IONM reduced transient RLN palsy (2.1% vs 3.4% with visual ID only) but not permanent palsy (0.4% vs 0.5%)
landmarkTrials.impact: IONM is a valuable adjunct but does not replace meticulous visual identification of the RLN
landmarkTrials.reference: Dralle H, et al. World J Surg. 2004;28(5):468-473

evidenceTable.recommendation: Total thyroidectomy is the preferred surgical option for bilateral multinodular goiter due to zero recurrence
evidenceTable.reference: Barczyński M, Langenbecks Arch Surg 2008; Mack LA, Am J Surg 2002

evidenceTable.recommendation: Retrosternal goiter requires pre-operative CT to assess extent and predict need for sternotomy
Iodized salt programs are the most effective public health intervention for endemic goiter
Levothyroxine suppression therapy is NOT recommended for benign multinodular goiter in euthyroid patients
Pre-operative laryngoscopy should be performed for all patients undergoing thyroid surgery to document vocal cord function
Visual identification of the recurrent laryngeal nerve is the gold standard; neuromonitoring is an adjunct, not a substitute
Post-operative calcium monitoring (at 6h, 12h, 24h) should be standard after total thyroidectomy
Radioactive iodine is an alternative to surgery for toxic MNG in elderly or high-risk patients
Lugol's iodine pre-operatively reduces thyroid vascularity in Graves' disease and large vascular goiters
evidenceTable.reference: Erbil Y, et al. World J Surg 2007

evidenceTable.recommendation: Universal salt iodization should be maintained to prevent recurrence of endemic goiter

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Patient with anterior neck swelling → History: duration, growth rate, compressive symptoms (dysphagia, dyspnea, voice change), hyper/hypothyroid symptoms, family history, iodine intake, goitrogenic foods (cassava/cabbage). Examination: thyroid palpation (size, consistency, nodules), movement with swallowing, retrosternal extension (Pemberton's sign), tracheal deviation, cervical lymph nodes, signs of hyper/hypothyroidism. → TSH, free T4 (T3 if TSH suppressed). If TSH suppressed: consider toxic goiter, perform scintigraphy. If TSH elevated/low normal: workup for hypothyroidism. → Thyroid Ultrasound: size, nodularity, TIRADS scoring of dominant nodules, retrosternal extension, tracheal assessment, lymph node survey. → If dominant nodule with suspicious features: FNA with Bethesda classification. → Assess compressive symptoms: compression score (dysphagia 0-3, dyspnea 0-3, voice change 0-3, retrosternal extension 0-3). Score ≥6: surgery indicated. Score 3-5: individualize. Score <3: observe. → CT Neck without contrast (or with contrast if surgery planned and no RAI in immediate future) — if retrosternal extension suspected, large goiter, or compressive symptoms. → Management: Asymptomatic euthyroid benign MNG → observation with iodine supplementation + annual US. Compressive symptoms or retrosternal extension → total thyroidectomy (cervical approach; sternotomy if below aortic arch). Toxic MNG → anti-thyroid drugs + surgery, or RAI if unfit. Graves' disease → medical therapy 12-18 months, then RAI or surgery. → Post-thyroidectomy: monitor calcium, voice, airway. Start Levothyroxine 1.6mcg/kg, adjust at 6-8 weeks.

managementPearls.context: The RLN is more at risk in large goiters
managementPearls.pearl: In large goiters, the RLN may be stretched, compressed, or displaced from its normal position in the tracheoesophageal groove. It can be splayed over the surface of a retrosternal nodule. The nerve may also be thinned and harder to see. Go slow, dissect meticulously, and use proximal identification at the thoracic inlet if the standard mid-gland identification is obscured.
managementPearls.evidence: Dralle H, et al. RLN in thyroid surgery. World J Surg 2004

managementPearls.context: Pemberton's sign is a surgical indication
managementPearls.pearl: A positive Pemberton's sign (facial congestion, cyanosis, and distress with arm elevation) indicates significant retrosternal compression. This is ALWAYS a surgical indication — these patients are at risk of acute airway obstruction from a minor bleed into the thyroid or progression of the goiter. Do not observe.

managementPearls.context: Total thyroidectomy for MNG — the recurrence argument
managementPearls.pearl: Subtotal thyroidectomy leaves behind thyroid tissue that can regenerate, especially in an iodine-deficient environment like Kenya. The recurrence rate after subtotal thyroidectomy is 10-30% at 10-15 years. Re-operative thyroid surgery has 5-10x higher RLN injury and hypoparathyroidism rates. Total thyroidectomy eliminates recurrence risk. Do the complete operation first time.

managementPearls.context: Retrosternal goiter — delivery through the neck
managementPearls.pearl: 85-90% of retrosternal goiters can be delivered through a cervical incision. Key maneuver: mobilize the superior pole first (frees the gland from above), then use a combination of blunt dissection with a finger and gentle traction with a Babcock clamp. If the gland does not come up easily, check for adhesions to the innominate artery or pleura. NEVER pull hard — you can avulse the RLN or innominate artery. Convert to sternotomy if needed.

Even with universal salt iodization, goiter persists in some Kenyan communities due to: non-iodized salt use (artisanal salt), cassava consumption, and poor iodine content in some commercial salts. Check urinary iodine (UI) in patients from endemic areas. UI <100mcg/L indicates deficiency requiring supplementation.

managementPearls.context: Lugol's iodine pre-op — when and why
managementPearls.pearl: Give Lugol's iodine (5-10 drops TDS) for 10 days before surgery for Graves' disease or large vascular goiters. It inhibits thyroid hormone release (Wolff-Chaikoff effect), reduces gland vascularity and blood loss by 30-40%, and makes the gland firmer and easier to dissect. Do NOT give it pre-op without controlling hyperthyroidism first with Carbimazole/PTU.
managementPearls.evidence: Erbil Y, et al. Lugol's solution before thyroidectomy. World J Surg 2007

operativeNuances.pitfall: Injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve where it passes through Berry's ligament — the ligament is dense and the nerve may be intimately adherent
operativeNuances.expertTip: Berry's ligament is the posteromedial suspensory ligament attaching the thyroid to the trachea. The RLN often passes THROUGH or immediately POSTERIOR to it. Techniques: (1) approach Berry's ligament from the LATERAL aspect after fully dissecting the RLN to its entry point at the cricothyroid, (2) use micro-scissors to divide the ligament in small bites, (3) use nerve stimulation to confirm RLN location before dividing any tissue in this area.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Parathyroid devascularization — the parathyroid blood supply is from the inferior thyroid artery, which is often ligated too proximally
operativeNuances.expertTip: NEVER ligate the main trunk of the inferior thyroid artery (ITA). Ligate only the terminal branches ON THE THYROID CAPSULE. The parathyroids receive their blood supply from the ITA — ligating the main trunk devascularizes them. Capsular dissection: dissect the ITA branches close to the thyroid surface, leaving the main ITA and its parathyroid branches intact.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Inadequate hemostasis from the superior thyroid vessels — the upper pole is highly vascular and retracts if cut, causing a difficult-to-control bleed in the upper field
operativeNuances.expertTip: The superior thyroid artery (external carotid branch) and vein run together at the upper pole. Ligate them INDIVIDUALLY close to the thyroid capsule (to protect the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve). Use a 'back-door' technique: dissect the space between the upper pole and the cricothyroid muscle, identify the nerve (EBSLN), then ligate the vessels under direct vision.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Incomplete sternotomy or wrong level — not extending the sternotomy far enough to expose the inferior extent of the goiter
operativeNuances.expertTip: Perform a partial upper median sternotomy (to the 2nd or 3rd intercostal space) for most retrosternal goiters. For goiters extending to the aortic arch or below, a full median sternotomy is needed. Place a rolled towel vertically behind the shoulders to extend the neck and promote delivery of the gland. The anesthetist must use a reinforced endotracheal tube.

operativeNuances.procedure: Emergency Thyroidectomy for Acute Airway Compromise
operativeNuances.pitfall: Inducing anesthesia in a patient with critical tracheal stenosis from goiter — the ET tube may not pass beyond the obstruction
operativeNuances.expertTip: Pre-op: CT to measure the narrowest tracheal diameter and distance from vocal cords. Technique: 'awake fiberoptic intubation' with spontaneous ventilation. Have a rigid bronchoscope and tracheostomy set open and ready. The most senior anesthetist and surgeon should be present. Consider extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) standby in extreme cases.

prognosis.mortality: Goiter-related mortality is very low (<0.1%) in elective cases. Emergency presentations (acute airway obstruction from hemorrhage into a cyst or retrosternal goiter) have 5-10% mortality. Thyroid surgery mortality: 0.1-0.3% for total thyroidectomy in experienced hands. Retrosternal goiter: 0.5-1% (higher if sternotomy needed). In Kenya, late presentation with airway compromise increases surgical risk.
prognosis.morbidity: Permanent hypoparathyroidism: 2-5% after total thyroidectomy, higher after central neck dissection (5-10%). Permanent RLN injury: 0.5-2%. Transient RLN palsy: 2-5%. Neck hematoma: 0.5-1% (requires emergency evacuation). Wound infection: 1-2%. Scar keloid: 10-20% in African patients. Hypothyroidism after total thyroidectomy: 100% (managed with Levothyroxine).
prognosis.recurrence: Total thyroidectomy: 0% recurrence. Subtotal thyroidectomy: 10-17% at 5 years, up to 30% at 15 years. In iodine-deficient regions, recurrence is even higher (up to 50%) due to ongoing TSH stimulation. Toxic nodular goiter: 5-10% recurrence after RAI at 5 years (new nodule formation).
prognosis.survival: Normal life expectancy after goiter surgery for benign disease. Even patients with permanent hypoparathyroidism have normal survival (quality of life is affected, not lifespan). Hyperthyroidism from toxic MNG/Graves' carries a 2-3x increased cardiovascular risk if untreated; after treatment, survival returns to population baseline.
prognosis.reference: Barczyński M, Langenbecks Arch Surg 2008; Zimmermann MB, Thyroid 2004; Dralle H, World J Surg 2004
